explicit_text: When my parents came to America in 1987, my father made sure to bring his super hip SONY stereo with him. My mother bought that brand new stereo back in 1984 while we were living in the Chiang Khong Refugee Camp, using some of her first earnings from sewing Paj Ntaub. It was the latest model and very popular in the camp, where neighbors would gather around to listen to the radio with my dad. In America, the stereo became a tool for recording voice messages to send to our relatives in Laos, making it a treasured family item.

explicit_text: My parents and I lived in the Chiang Khong Refugee Camp before coming to America. It was there that my mother sewed Paj Ntaub to earn income, and with some of that money, she bought my dad a brand new SONY stereo in 1984. The stereo was very popular in the camp, and neighbors would gather around to listen to the radio with my father. This camp was a significant place in our lives before we relocated to the United States in 1987.

explicit_text: Our family originally comes from Laos, where many of our relatives still live. After moving to the United States in 1987, my parents used the SONY stereo to record voice messages on cassette tapes to send back to family in Laos. I remember my mother having me record messages addressed to her sister and cousin in Laos, trying to keep our family connected across the distance. Laos has always been an important part of our identity and a place we long to stay connected with.

That country has always been an important part of our identity and a place we long to stay connected with.

explicit_text: My parents moved to the United States of America in 1987, bringing with them important belongings like my father's SONY stereo. In America, the stereo was used differently than before; it became a device to record voice messages on cassette tapes to send to relatives still living in Laos. Growing up in the U.S., I participated in these recordings even though I was young and unsure what to say. The United States became our new home, but we maintained strong ties to our family and culture through these recordings.
